anchor.snippet: a map projection is any of a broad set of transformations
All projections of a sphere on a plane necessarily distort the surface
Theorema Egregium proved that a sphere's surface cannot be represented on a plane without distortion
Nicolas Tissot described how to construct an ellipse that illustrates the amount and orientation of the components of distortion
A surface that can be unfolded or unrolled into a plane or sheet without stretching, tearing or shrinking is called a developable surface
The cylinder , cone and the plane are all developable surfaces

annotations.label: Sphere and ellipsoid are not developable

anchor.snippet: The sphere and ellipsoid do not have developable surfaces
The aspect describes how the developable surface is placed relative to the globe
Tangent means the surface touches but does not slice through the globe
Tangent and secant lines ( standard lines ) are represented undistorted
The central meridian is the meridian to which the globe is rotated before projecting
A globe is the only way to represent the Earth with constant scale throughout the entire map in all directions
This is equivalent to preservation of angles, the defining characteristic of a conformal map
This is the defining characteristic of an equidistant projection

annotations.label: No projection is both equal-area and conformal

anchor.snippet: it is impossible to construct a map projection that is both equal-area and conformal
A normal cylindrical projection is any projection in which meridians are mapped to equally spaced vertical lines

annotations.label: Cylindrical projections stretch east-west by secant of latitude

anchor.snippet: The amount of stretch is the same at any chosen latitude on all cylindrical projections, and is given by the secant of the latitude

annotations.label: Mercator: north-south stretching equals sec φ

anchor.snippet: The east-west scale matches the north-south scale: conformal cylindrical or Mercator
North-south distances neither stretched nor compressed (1): equirectangular projection
North-south compression equals the cosine of the latitude
A transverse cylindrical projection is a cylindrical projection that in the tangent case uses a great circle along a meridian
An oblique cylindrical projection aligns with a great circle, but not the equator and not a meridian
Pseudocylindrical projections represent the central meridian as a straight line segment
Sinusoidal , which was the first pseudocylindrical projection developed
The HEALPix projection combines an equal-area cylindrical projection in equatorial regions with the Collignon projection in polar areas
The term conic projection is used to refer to any projection in which meridians are mapped to equally spaced lines radiating out from the apex
Equidistant conic , which keeps parallels evenly spaced along the meridians
Albers conic , which adjusts the north-south distance between non-standard parallels to compensate for the east-west stretching
Lambert conformal conic , which adjusts the north-south distance between non-standard parallels to equal the east-west stretching
Bonne , an equal-area projection on which most meridians and parallels appear as curved lines
Werner cordiform , upon which distances are correct from one pole
Azimuthal projections have the property that directions from a central point are preserved
The gnomonic projection displays great circles as straight lines
The orthographic projection maps each point on the Earth to the closest point on the plane
Near-sided perspective projection, which simulates the view from space at a finite distance
The General Perspective projection can be constructed by using a point of perspective outside the Earth
The stereographic projection , which is conformal, can be constructed by using the tangent point's antipode as the point of perspective
Distance from the tangent point on the map is proportional to surface distance on the Earth
Distance from the tangent point on the map is proportional to straight-line distance through the Earth
Logarithmic azimuthal is constructed so that each point's distance from the center of the map is the logarithm of its distance from the tangent point
Polyhedral map projections use a polyhedron to subdivide the globe into faces
Conformal , or orthomorphic, map projections preserve angles locally
Equal-area maps preserve area measure, generally distorting shapes in order to do so
An equidistant projection preserves distances from one or two special points to all other points
Two-point equidistant : Two "control points" are arbitrarily chosen by the map maker
Great circles are displayed as straight lines: Gnomonic projection
Direction to a fixed location B (the bearing at the starting location A of the shortest route) corresponds to the direction on the map from A to B
Compromise projections give up the idea of perfectly preserving metric properties
